era_context: The mid-15th century was a period of significant political and military transition. The Ottoman Empire was consolidating power in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans, the Hundred Years' War was in its final stages, the Italian Renaissance was flourishing, and the Ming Dynasty maintained stability in China. This was a transitional period between medieval and early modern political structures.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records fragmentary)
Central Asia and Steppe regions (limited documentation)

events.title: Death of John Hunyadi, Hungarian military leader
events.description: John Hunyadi, one of the greatest Hungarian military commanders and a leading figure in the resistance against Ottoman expansion, died in 1456. His death marked the end of an era of Hungarian military dominance in the Balkans. Hunyadi's son, Matthias Corvinus, would inherit his legacy and become an even greater king, but Hunyadi's death represented a significant loss of military leadership at a critical moment in Hungarian-Ottoman relations.
key_figures: Matthias Corvinus (son, future King of Hungary)
events.certainty_note: John Hunyadi's death is well-documented in Hungarian records. The date is certain.
cross_references: Led to 1458-001 (Matthias Corvinus crowned King of Hungary)

events.title: Siege of Belgrade by Ottoman forces
events.description: The Ottomans under Mehmed II besieged Belgrade in 1456, seeking to capture this strategic fortress on the Danube. The siege was relieved by Hungarian forces under John Hunyadi, who led a successful defense that prevented Ottoman capture of the city. This victory was one of the last major Hungarian successes against the Ottomans and temporarily halted Ottoman expansion into Central Europe. The relief of Belgrade became a symbol of Christian resistance against Ottoman expansion.
events.certainty_note: The Siege of Belgrade is well-documented in both Ottoman and Hungarian sources. The date is certain.
cross_references: Part of broader Ottoman expansion in the Balkans

events.title: Gutenberg's printing press produces the Gutenberg Bible
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg's movable type printing press produced the Gutenberg Bible (also known as the 42-line Bible) around 1456. This was one of the first major works printed using movable type in Europe and is considered one of the most important technological innovations in human history. The printing press revolutionized the dissemination of information and knowledge, contributing significantly to the Renaissance and the Protestant Reformation.
key_figures: Johannes Gutenberg (inventor of the printing press)

sources.name: Surviving copies of the Gutenberg Bible
Later accounts of Gutenberg's life and work

events.certainty_note: The Gutenberg Bible is dated to c. 1454–1456 by modern scholarship. The exact year is uncertain, but 1456 is a conventional date.
cross_references: Part of broader Renaissance technological innovation
Led to 1517-001 (Protestant Reformation, enabled by printing press)

events.title: Joan of Arc canonization proceedings (Nullification Trial)
events.description: In 1456, the Nullification Trial (also called the Trial of Rehabilitation) of Joan of Arc was conducted in Rouen, France. This trial overturned her 1431 conviction for heresy and witchcraft, declaring her innocent. The trial was initiated by Joan's mother and supported by the French crown. This rehabilitation of Joan's memory was significant for French national identity and for the Catholic Church's recognition of her sanctity.
key_figures: Joan of Arc (subject of the trial, executed 1431)

sources.name: Trial records of the Nullification Trial

events.certainty_note: The Nullification Trial is well-documented in trial records and French sources. The date is certain.

events.title: Hundred Years' War in final stages
events.description: The Hundred Years' War between England and France was in its final stages in 1456. The English had lost most of their continental possessions and were facing internal conflict (the Wars of the Roses). France was consolidating its power under Louis XI. The war would effectively end in the late 1450s, though formal peace was not declared until 1475.
events.certainty_note: The war's final stages are documented in English and French records, though the exact year of conclusion is somewhat conventional.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the Gutenberg Bible is uncertain; modern scholarship places it between 1454 and 1456, with 1456 being a conventional date. Some sources date it earlier or later. The Nullification Trial of Joan of Arc is well-documented, but some sources refer to it as the 'Trial of Rehabilitation' or 'Retrial,' which can cause confusion with other proceedings. John Hunyadi's death date is sometimes given as 1456 or 1457 depending on the source, though 1456 is more commonly accepted.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1456 is rich in European sources, particularly from Hungary, France, England, and the Holy Roman Empire. German sources on Gutenberg are limited; most accounts are later compilations. Ottoman sources are available but often later compilations. Chinese sources are abundant but focus on court-centered events. African and American sources are virtually absent from the surviving written record. The bias toward political, military, and technological events is pronounced; economic, cultural, and social developments are less systematically recorded.

graph_edges.note: John Hunyadi's death led to his son Matthias Corvinus's coronation as King of Hungary in 1458.
The Siege of Belgrade was part of the broader Ottoman expansion that began with the fall of Constantinople in 1453.
The printing press enabled the rapid dissemination of Protestant ideas, contributing to the Reformation in 1517.
The Nullification Trial overturned Joan of Arc's 1431 execution and conviction.
The final stages of the Hundred Years' War led to the Treaty of Picquigny in 1475.
